summary: Joe Reis argues that data modeling is evolving from siloed, single-discipline approaches toward a multi-disciplinary practice he calls 'Mixed Model Arts.' Drawing an analogy to how MMA fighters must now master multiple fighting styles rather than relying on boxing or wrestling alone, he contends that data professionals must become proficient across relational, dimensional, ML/AI, and semantic data modeling techniques to remain effective.
key_points: Data modeling is moving from siloed expertise (Kimball, Data Vault, relational) to cross-disciplinary proficiency
AI adoption is forcing companies to re-examine data quality and structure, giving data modeling renewed importance
Data-powered products require modeling data differently across software, analytics, and ML/AI use cases simultaneously
The future belongs to 'Productland' (data products) rather than 'Enterpriseland' (internal reports)
Roles will evolve toward cross-functional titles like 'data product engineer'
money_quotes: Being one-dimensional in MMA is a recipe for disaster... People working with data in a one-dimensional way are similar to combat sports before the UFC.
Ignore this, and you're like the boxer in UFC 1 wearing a single boxing glove. You're not going to win at the game of data.
AI without good-quality data means you'll do dumb things more quickly.
In general, Productland is the future and Enterpriseland is a dead-end.
Stop obsessing over specific siloed techniques. Get to know the big ideas in data modeling across disciplines and use cases.
key_insight: Just as MMA forced martial artists to become multi-disciplinary fighters, the convergence of AI, data products, and cross-functional use cases is forcing data practitioners to master multiple data modeling approaches rather than specializing in a single technique.
